1. The Trust Flywheel - Jeremy Haynes.mp4
Give me one big clap if you're ready to get started. Little more succinct. One more time. Let's count it down, okay? Three, two, one, clap. Okay, yeah, there we go. Good. All right, so you see this in front of me? Or I should say behind me? This is what I refer to as the trust flywheel. This has been a very consistent theme that has come up time and time again over these last several weeks with you guys. And I wanted to put together a solution that's going to solve the problem. Allow me to articulate what the problem is. First of all, raise your hand if you've seen show rates dip a little bit to start the year. Like you've seen a little less. Consistency and a little less of a show rate.
It's starting to erode a little bit. Yeah, raise your hand real high if that's you. Yeah. Why do you think that is? Anybody, throw it out. What do you think the guess is? Why are people not showing up as much to start the year? Yeah. Who else? Certain about the upcoming political climate. I mean, I guess. I mean, just to give you some counter perspective, who here is excited about the upcoming political environment? Yeah, I was going to say, I think that's wrong. Okay, anybody else?
Okay, so... Look, here's my take. There's a concept from a great book. I refer to this book all the time. Everybody should have this book and reread it, at least on an annual basis. It's called Breakthrough Advertising by a guy named Eugene Schwartz, written in the 50s. And in this book, he talks about this concept called market sophistication. Okay, so raise your hand real quick if you run a call funnel. Just look around real quick. Keep your hand high if you're on call funnel. How many of you right now in this exact moment run the hammer them strategy for your call funnel? Raise your hand. Okay, now let's use the example that your specific demographic has a little bit of crossover to somebody else that sells something else in this room.
Just to also give you some perspective, you guys are amongst some of the most sophisticated advertisers that exist. As you know, when you come in here, are you running the hammer them strategy already? No. Are you doing the most sophisticated things there are to do when it comes to increasing show rates, getting people to show up better framed? No. You do all these things after. Okay, so in your specific room right now, it might seem like there's a high concentration of people doing it. When in reality, every one of your competitors operate like dipshits. They don't do any of that stuff. They do classic email marketing reminders. They do very outdated best practices when it comes to trying to get people to show up framed. So.
At the same time, I'm looking at a lot of the problems that you guys consistently face. I'm talking to one of the guys that I consistently print million-dollar months with, this guy named Josh Troy. And Josh, he'll be speaking at our Q2 Mastermind. For all those who have been in here a while, you guys know I love Josh. He's a very serious person. He's probably my favorite, like, sales agency that exists. And Josh has... You know, very interesting perspectives on VSLs, most recently. So we have a lot of clients. We run really two to three funnels in almost every client that we have. We run a monthly webinar. Where we typically spend upwards of 100 to 300K per webinar to get tens of thousands of people to register for these things.
And by the time they show up, they're typically very well framed because they've had the hammer them that retargets the shit out of them. They've had the trust flywheel, which I'll walk you through that we've been testing on a lot of our clients. They just show up ready to buy. But, you know, we can only run the webinar once a month. And the issue with the webinar is... It creates a big spike the day of and the days that follow. And there's just a tremendous drop off after that. You don't get a lot of consistency in calls and high-ticket sales throughout the entire month. A lot of you might think the solution is to then increase the frequency of the webinar. But what we found time and time again is that erodes.
The overall revenue that comes from each webinar that's executed. So we actually see a diminishing return the more webinars that we do. So we always have to run a call funnel. How many of you think that people watch 100% of a VSO? Yeah, good. Thank God none of you raised your hand. That was a trick question. That was a booby trap. I was going to publicly execute you if you said yes. Okay, anyway. So as we know, people do not watch... Justin, by the way, there's a seat right over here if you want to sit. Okay, so long story short. Nobody watches the entirety of a VSL on a call funnel page. We all agree to that? Do we all agree to that? Okay.
So if somebody watches a webinar, they watch upwards of 90 minutes to like three hours worth of framing material. If somebody on average watches like two to three minutes of a VSL, there's obviously a tremendous gap. In the amount of consumption that somebody has prior to the conversion event, which is the sales call happening, the intention of the hammer them strategy when it was conceptualized was: I need to get content in front of people in an extremely high frequency in an extremely short duration of time. To get that same consumption rate that would otherwise happen in a webinar or a longer format consumption. Does that make sense? Okay, so here's two things that I did. Number one, and this is a very important thing I want to make sure you all take note on right away.

We started taking long-form content from all these brands that we work with that have it, okay? And we started doing the same thing with the hammer them strategy. All we did was double the quantity of content. And as ridiculous as that sounds, because it's already 30 to 50 pieces of short-form content, we loaded up on average about 30 different long-form pieces of content.
In addition to all the short-form content that's already there. I want to be very clear when I say this tactically. I did a second hammer them campaign for the long form content, specifically. Okay, you can put it within the existing short form hammer them campaign. But you have to ensure that you do an ad set budget if you put it within the same campaign. Because otherwise, if it's on a CBO level, a campaign budget optimization level, it'll always bias towards the short form and the long form will get snuffed and we'll never get any reach. I always like to post it direct to the feed because, just like what Andrew said, like there'll be people who go there and consume all that stuff anyway. Might as well have it there rather than, you know, hide it behind the ads manager and make people get targeted by it to see it. You run the same exclusions. So you run a three-second exclusion on anybody who watches that video. That way, they get hit by other videos to follow up. thing that we found is that, if we tried to exclude people who watched longer durations, they would get hit with that same video again and again and again.
We don't want that. As an example, let's say you don't give a fuck about one particular video that keeps targeting you. If I don't exclude it after you watch three seconds and you never watch three seconds, you'll never actually see all the other content that there is to get targeted by. Does that make sense? Okay. Increased the consumption before the call. To get that call to be a much higher close rate as a result of that consumption happening before the call occurred. Does that make sense? That's what we're seeing as the trend right now. Now, literally this month, today, is if we get the consumption rate for more than half of the audience to be at least about two hours worth of content, we see a substantial difference.
We went, just to give this some perspective. I won't say this client's name so I can share their stats. We had a client that was spending about $400,000 a month. This particular client, we took from about a 9% VSL call funnel close rate. to a 24% close rate when we added just the long form hammer them content as an adjustment to their call funnel. Does that make sense? It was profound and it was the singular variable that made the difference. Okay. On other clients, it was marginal. Like we saw single digit percentage increases anywhere as low as about 5%, as high as about 9% in terms of the single digit. impacts that it had on three out of four people that we tried it on.
There was one outlier, and there were three people that had single-digit percentages all above 5% for the impacts that the long-form hammer them strategy had. Does that make sense? Okay. Like, hey, you've been fucked by other people before. Like, I'm not going to fuck you, okay? Like, I'm the right one. Sit down if you address that.

You see tremendous lifts in close rates. You see tremendous lifts in AOV, as an example. For those who stay standing, why not address that? Why would you not put something together? That talks to people. About how they've been fucked before. If they're gonna come to you... Like, if you were gonna date somebody, as an example... I'm sure you guys have all had this experience. Where they bring up their past.
And they're like, 'Oh, dude, are you going to hurt me like this past person did?' You know? You've had that happen, right? Think about that. It's like you got to have that part of the conversation in order to get into that relationship. You see what I mean? All right, everybody sit down. If we don't address it and it remains unspoken, there's always that uncertainty that sits in the back of the other person's mind. Does that make sense? Some of your people that come through your funnels right now that don't show up, they don't show up because they didn't have their real objection addressed. That's sitting in the back of their mind. And here's the thing, right?
When we do advertising at scale or any kind of marketing at scale, are we getting the opportunity to talk to every single person before they show up? What makes a great marketer? Somebody who can successfully guess the conversation that needs to happen without having communication with the other party. If I successfully tell you the things that you're thinking... I dramatically increase the probability of that transaction that we want occurring. Right? If I fail to communicate what's actually on the mind of the person that you're trying to convert, they might just ghost us and not show up. Does that make sense? This is where I created the trust flywheel. It starts with the real objections. This is an exercise now. Get out your pen and paper. The real objections are where we start.
You have to right now come up with the actual things. Remember what makes a great marketer— being able to successfully guess. What the counterparty's thinking? That they're not telling you that's currently fucking you out of getting money. Does that make sense? What's the real reason they're not buying your stuff? What's the guess that you're going to take? For what is currently preventing people from showing up to that call, from showing up to that webinar? Think about this through this perspective. Your salespeople likely tell you some of these things that come up occasionally. You may be occasionally, I'm talking back in the day, way back in the day, my last marketing job where I worked with Grant Cardone. We had this rule. If one person said it, a thousand people think it.
We went with that rule. And it worked almost all the time. If one person says it, like I'll give you a great example with you guys. I remember there was a guy who dropped out. And I was like, 'Why in the fuck would you drop out? You can make so much money being a part of this group. Like, you're the perfect person for it. And his reason was... Dude, there's just so much shit to consume, I'm falling behind on my consumption.' So as a result of falling behind on my consumption... I just, I don't feel like, like there's a point of me continuing to be a part of it because I'm not like, I'm not actually consuming everything.
And that was such a valid point to me that I decided to invest in AI developers to fucking create Telegram chat bots that are gonna on a weekly basis. create a summary of all that shit that happens in the group chat, from videos to the audio notes to the actual things that are all written. It'll create a summary. And it'll create an AI Jeremy voice that you can listen to if you prefer that to go through the summary as well. We take this is going to take about another month and a half, by the way, to finish this one. But this will be cool when it happens. Every single weekly call it's like a 30-minute to two-hour call.
There'll be executive summaries that are short, executive summaries that are longer, and the actual weekly video itself that you can go through for consumption on that. That's a good example of me listening to a single person's feedback and assuming it's a problem that all of you experience. Is that safe to say that most of you will have some benefit as a result of those actions and those improvements? See what I mean? So it's like, again, back to you and your business. What is the real objection? And obviously, it's not one. There's a real set of reasons that are currently holding people back that you're not speaking about. You have nothing that addresses it. Okay and let me give you some examples so you can think with it.
There was one inner circle member—well, technically they're business partners— they're out in Dubai. And these guys. One day they were like, 'Dude, our show rate's just fucking continuing to go down.' They were taking every action. Hammer them, updated marketing automation, selfie videos, every fucking best practice under the sun. These guys were doing. They didn't see any successful impact from that. So I Google one day their company name and the word Reddit. There's a Reddit thread from some people who didn't even buy their product that just went through their sales process. And we're like, 'Yeah, dude, I mean, it's super expensive.' It costs $12, 500 upfront and then, after you get results, it costs another $12,500.
And then some people who have never even bought their course jump in and are like, 'Oh yeah, dude, all courses are a fucking scam.' Like, never buy a course, you know, like that's never going to work for anybody. Date that their show rate tanked. Correlated to the exact week that that Reddit thread was initially published. They had nothing that addressed that. How many people did something as simple as looking up their company name for a 25, 000 offer see that Reddit thread and just said, 'I'm not fucking showing up for that. I'm not wasting my time. Obviously, more than half of their people. Their show rate went into the 30s. So you could say that high 60% of people. Either, first of all, this weren't going to show up anyway, which there's always a percentage of, of course.
We won't ever neglect that. But a high quantity of people, their show rate was historically 70%. So you're telling me that 40% of people saw one Reddit thread and they just were like, 'I'm out; not wasting time.' As soon as that company came out with a video, literally just going through the Reddit thread and showing, oh, these people never even bought from us. Let me give you a whole bunch of people who bought from us and their stories and how it works. Just became a giant testimonial video. And they literally named the video addressing this Reddit thread and they named the Reddit thread what the Reddit thread was titled. So it would SEO right alongside the Reddit thread, you know, how when you Google something, there's the YouTube videos that show up first.
Take out your phone right now. Go to Google. I'll give you 30 seconds to do it. Once you're at Google, I want you to type in the word Jeremy Haynes and scam. And I want you to just press Enter, and I want you to see what comes up. Tell me the first things you see. Somebody just start yelling it out once you see things. What's on Twitter? Who wrote that tweet? Oh, wow. What a surprise, huh? So you're telling me I can rank on Twitter by typing the words Jeremy Haynes scam and then literally click the tweet. Look how fucking well written that tweet is. Yeah. Click around. Now, listen to this. If there was anything that ever actually came up when you searched that. I would make a video just addressing it.
Because that would prevent people from buying things if I didn't. But if I did, what does that say if I'm willing to make a video and address it? What does that say in comparison? That you're willing to confront it? Fuck yeah. You see? And what difference does that make in the mind of a buyer who's on the fence and doesn't trust you yet? People don't come in with default trust. Trust, okay? People have been fucked over in their lives. I'll give you a great example. I recently bought about $40,000 of art, okay? I went in there. There low balling the shit out of this guy. He'd have a piece of art for like 28 grand.
I'd be like, 'I'll give you 10.' And he'd look at me for a second, and he'd be like, 'All right.' Like, 'Fine.' And I'm feeling like the fucking man over here. I'm like, 'Ah, dude, I'm fucking finessing this guy.' I'm getting great art from my house. Like, this is awesome. And... I spent that 40 grand in about two months, okay? Trusted the guy by default. I operated with a bias that I'm in a high-trust society, and I can just... Trust anybody no matter what. That's my typical worldview. I don't like to operate in a pessimistic world. As what it seems like most of you operate with that as well, since you didn't even Google me before you bought my shit. Okay? So you must agree.
Well, this guy hits me up one day, late December, and he says, you know, I'm trying to buy the building that my gallery's in, and I need to raise money. So I'm going to sell Andy Warhol's at wholesale pricing. And I was like, sick, like a low-ball opportunity. What do you got? And he had these money signs. For all those who don't know, Andy Warhol was worth $100 million when he died. He asked 15 people. He said, what do you think I should paint next? And they said, well, what do you love? And he was like, I love money. So he decided to paint dollar signs. I resonated with that. So he had these four dollar sign paintings that were supposedly from Andy Warhol.
They were limited edition 28 prints, which aligned perfectly to what the internet said. The internet price for each one of these— somebody want to take a guess—was a hundred to a hundred and thirty K a piece on the internet. Okay? He had them listed in his store for twenty-five grand a piece. For what he was willing to sell them to you— if you just walked in and tried to buy them— he offered them all four to me for twenty-four grand. And I low-balled them. I said, look, I don't really even give a shit to buy these. I mean, this is a random purchase for me. I'll offer you 15 for all four. If that somehow makes a difference to you buying this building and you're willing to get fucked like that.
I'll fuck you, sure. And he was like, 'I go and take a shower.' By the time I get out of the shower, I had a message with a Stripe checkout link. Now that one threw me through a set of red flags where I was like, 'You know, let me do a little research here.' I Google the guy's name and the word 'scam.' and I see a fucking New York Times article about how this guy in 2015 went to prison for paint forgery. And then that wasn't even what the New York Times article was about. It was about a family that he finessed for $6 million. $6 million, selling what? Andy fucking Warhol. Dog, I got hypnotized over that trauma. That broke my worldview of high trust.
It took me to a perspective of, like, is everybody trying to secretly me? Do you know how much that's shifted my buying behavior since? Instead of being able to walk in somewhere and just be like, 'Oh, 10K for a piece of art? Like, sure, here you go.' I Google the fuck out of everything now. It jaded me. It made me reconsider my buying behavior. I made it to 30. Before that happened. I am a part of the population like I assume you are that's willing to take some big fucking risks. Consistently. And I'm fully willing to get fucked as a result of the risks I take. Right? The people you sell to. Do you think they're like that too? Let's assume that it's dialed down a few notches, right?
Are they as ballsy as you? Are they as willing to just... RIP OVER. Untold amount of money. Without doing some basic research? Probably not. What are they going to want to know? Let's take your business, Shane. You operate in the Amazon coaching space. What happened? We have a guy in here right now, Mr. Ryan Carroll. His one company took $50 million out of the market and vaporized it. Just gone. Like a magician. Ryan, the magician. Do you have any kind of video for that one example? And by the way, how many examples are there, Shane? How many different examples are there of automation companies that absolutely fucking host people? How many do you think there are?
So, if there's more than a dozen, you better tell me right now that you have a video addressing how people can trust you as an Amazon course seller when they just got absolutely fucking rinsed for hundreds of millions of dollars as a consumer category. How's that video performing? And does it address the real objections? The real objection is, how do I know this isn't a scam and I won't lose my money? See, and you're going to make some surface-level video where it's like, 'Look, guys, my name's Shane. You know, I do. I do great business. You know what I mean? You can trust me. Don't worry about, hey, by the way, here's like some random testimonials I cherry-picked, putting the video for you here. You can trust me. I'm your guy.
Show up to the car. Is it something like that, Shane? Or does it address the real shit that they actually have on their mind? On the surface. Let me give you an example of what the FTC considers compliance. The FTC has this document. You heard about it recently. One of your buyers asked you for it. What was that document called, Shane? Say it loud for everybody so they can all hear it. I forget the time. Say what you think it's called. So there's this little document that the FTC likes people to have when you're doing a make money offer that openly states the probability of success with whatever your claim is. So if you make any kind of income claim, or if you have a testimonial that you cherry pick, first of all, a testimonial better be the true average.
average result. Okay? If you guys have ever raised your hand real quick, have you ever seen me market something like this on the internet where I'm like, 'Hey, we're giving away a million dollar a month trophy'? Have you ever seen me market that? Have you seen a tweet about that? Have you seen a YouTube video about that yet? Okay, well, do yourself a favor, go to my Twitter account real quick. It's one of the most recent tweets. Somebody open up Twitter real fast. Go to my Twitter. And then, whoever does it, bring me your phone. I'll read it aloud to you to give you an example of a compliant way to make such an audacious claim. The statistical probability of hitting $1 million a month.
To hit $10 million a year, according to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, is 0. 1%. Are we giving away $3 million a month trophies from one month worth of earnings in this group? Yeah. Yeah, we are. Is the statistical probability of... our group hitting a million dollars a month higher than the average of the regular business owner? Absolutely it is. There's like 15 of you now that hit a million dollars a month and that you consistently hit a million dollars a month. Does that mean that the probability increases? No! It's 0. 1%. That means, like Purell, there's a 99. 99% chance that you don't ever fucking see one of these trophies. Does that make sense? Somebody who brought up the tweet. Who brought it up?
Let me see. Here's the tweet, you ready? I literally start the tweet with earnings disclaimer. Jeremy's Inner Circle does not promise you in any way, shape, or form you will hit $1 million a month as a result of joining into the program. Your chances of hitting $10 million a year, according to the U. S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, is 0. 1%. That means you have a 99. 99% chance of not hitting $10 million. Million dollars a year, let alone 12 million dollars a year at 1 million a month, are there several people in the group doing it? Yes, does that mean the average result of the member that joins is a million a month? Absolutely not. Not even close, as a matter of fact.
The average member of the group currently sits around $200,000 to $300,000 a month in gross revenue for their businesses. This by no means represents that you will even get to that level as a result of joining into the program. We simply provide information, lessons, and resources from our top clients who have gotten the biggest results and hand them down to the members. Income claims or income representations that are made when joining into Jeremy's inner circle. As stated by law, we cannot and do not make any guarantees about your ability. To get results or earn any money with our ideas, information, programs, or strategies. We don't know you. And besides, your results in life are up to you.
Then I say, congratulations to our three Jeremy's Inner Circle members that are getting their million dollar a month trophies this weekend at the Mastermind. I don't know. Are you that compliant? Yeah, if you put that through an FDCAI tracker, it says there's a score of 100 out of 100 on the compliance spectrum. Let me ask you a question. Does that make sense? Okay, let's continue to number two, time investment. Edward, you tell me I can fuck the IRS and save myself some money. Boy, do I want to do that. But you know what I don't want to do? I don't want to spend, I hate the fucking IRS. I hate my taxes. There's not anything you could try to convince me of that would make me say, 'I want to also spend time in addition to money with you.' There's not anything you could say. So think about that. That's something that you actually have to address. You know, one thing just recently that blew my mind. I spent, everybody know WP Engine, the company that hosts WordPress sites. So I had a developer that we built a site with.
They told me to go sign up for WP Engine. We did. It was a great experience, the first purchase. I was able to sign up online, give them my money, and that was it. Well, the second site that I needed to buy, do you know what they made me do? They made me call them. Holy shit, was I mad. I didn't even know why I was mad. I just couldn't believe. I was like, I have to call you to buy a second site? What? So I make the call because I have to get the second site. And you know what this fucking worm on the other side of the phone tells me? He says, Jeremy, you actually have a rep that you've got to talk to.
The earliest they're available is tomorrow morning at 11 a. m. What? I have a rep? Who? And they're like, 'Oh, you've actually never met the person.' What? And I have to talk to him? And they were like, 'Yeah, in the earliest they're available tomorrow at 11 a. m.' And I'm like, 'No, dude, I'm not doing that.' There's not a chance in hell I'm going to spend time in addition to money with you. I'll sign up under a new email before I do that. And the guy's trying to prevent me at all costs from doing that. He's like, 'No, no, no, no.' Like, just don't worry. Like, we'll get you on the phone.' I'll schedule it right now. And I'm like, 'Dog,' I'm hanging up.
Like, I'm not talking to you any longer. And I hang up the call. And then I navigate to this part of the website where I do see I can buy. And right away, you know what that did for my experience on WP Engine? I fucking hate that company now. I'm like, 'So I had this guy on the phone that represents their business that told me I had to talk to somebody in order to buy. Well, that's obviously their incentive, right? Like they probably would make money if I bought through that person and maybe they'd try to upsell me on some other shit. But in reality, they didn't even say.' They literally told me verbatim, you cannot buy online for this second site. You have to go through this other person to buy.
They fucking lied to me. That was a lie. I could buy on the site. Think about that. So they fucking lied to me and they made me try to get on the phone. Why was I really mad about that? Somebody say it. There was no expectation set that I would ever have to spend time with them too. My expectation was I only have to spend money with you. If they would have just said, 'Hey, by the way, man, if you ever want to buy a second sight, you just got to jump on the phone with us.' I wouldn't have been pissed at all. I'd have been like, 'Oh, okay, cool.' Because the expectation was set. Does that make sense? So when I say. The time investment.
This is more of an expectation. Okay, because it's not just money. It's not. Like when we close people on raising capital, this is one of my favorite niches currently. You know what I tell them in the sales process? I say, 'Dude, for you to raise this money off these leads we're going to generate, you're going to have to call, text, and email people 20 times before they even answer the first time. You're going to have to have 12 meetings before they actually give you money. It's going to take. Three to six months.' Before they give you that cash, you're gonna have to develop like 30 trust assets. Like, you're going to have to share your books. You're going to have to make videos going through your entire, like, backlog of people who have ever given you money in the returns.
You're going to have to be detailed in a video about projects that have failed in the past. That's what I tell people when they're about to give us money to work with them as an agency in that niche. It usually takes less than that for them to raise the capital. But that's the expectation I set. That sounds fair, right? They have to know. That yeah, I can generate you a fuckload of accredited investor leads. They'll have the minimum you want them to have. There'll be all kinds of people who come in for much more than that. But it's gonna take this in order to get them to close. If I did not set that expectation, I talked to a guy. It's just crazy, by the way. His name's Ryan.
And Ryan had apparently bought during a master internet marketing sale in 2023, where I gave away a 30-minute call. And he booked it this last week in 2025. Which is hilarious, by the way. But I digress. Ryan gets on the phone and he's like, dude, I've had 12 of these raising capital clients and I've fucking failed with all 12 of them. Like I generate the leads really well. I get them everything that they need and then they just fail. Like, and they keep firing me. And I'm like, well, what are you telling them the expectation is for like what it's going to take to get these people close? And he's like, dude, I'm just telling him to call him a few times. And I'm like, well, I couldn't be further from the truth.
You know? So one guy fails miserably, all my raising capital clients are fucking pumped. They don't give a shit it takes three to six months. Why? Yeah, because I told them. I told them up front before they gave me any money. Does that make sense? You know what else people would like to know? The risk. What is my risk of doing a deal with you? And I'm talking the real risks. Again, not like the surface-level bullshit that you're trying to make up. Like real legitimate risks of doing the deal with you. Dude, I tell every client when they work with us, I say, look, your probability of hitting a million dollars a month is technically the highest when you work with us as a client if we take you on.
However, that does not in any way, shape, or form guarantee it. Every person who wants to work with us wants to hit a million dollars a month. Okay. I tell them point blank. I say, first of all, I have stories where people have done it in 30 days. I have stories where people have hit two and a half million dollars in 30 days. Does that mean you'll hit two and a half million dollars in 30 days? It could take you fucking years. Okay? James, you're a good example. What was your revenue at at the beginning of 2024 on a month-to-month basis? Between 150... He was at 150 to 200 at the beginning of the year. What did you finish 2024 with in cash collected revenue in the month of December?
He hit a million in December. Does that mean that you'll hit a million in a year? No, it could take two years, three years, okay? It could take 30 fucking days. It's an unbound amount of time. Then I have to tell them the risks. I have to say, let me be extremely clear. When you enter into an agency relationship with us, we have you spend $50,000 in 30 days, plus you give me $15,000. You're going to spend $65,000 in 30 days, okay? You're going to look at that on your Amex bill, and you're going to shit yourself if we don't make that money back. Okay. But that is a possibility that could occur. I say, here's the risks. Okay.
Risk number one, I might not be able to get you the lead you're looking for. Risk number two, your salespeople might blow this fucking deal up and just flop miserably. There might be something that occurs where, like, you know, we don't we don't see something online that people are looking up that tanks your show rate all of a sudden. It's like, I have to go through all the legitimate risks that are correlated to my offers. Does that make sense? Like, as an example, Yash, you're a sponsor over here for Fan Basis. You're saying to me on the phone that everybody gets a percentage that's less than Stripe. This guy's sitting right here. Hit me up goes, 'Oh, I'm at 5%.' I said, 'Well, make sure you talk to Yash while you're here.' Because again, the expectation you set with me in terms of risk is that they all get a fee that's less than Stripe.
Then I'll go out and I'll say that. But it's like, is that the reality of the situation? It's like, well, no, who knows? He did 1 million in a month. He's at 5. 5%. That seems whack, you know? But again, if you market on the front end that people have a risk that they could be higher, well, it might not be bad. You see, because the expectation is set of what can actually happen relative to all kinds of conditions and variables, right? You guys see where I'm going with this? The real risks, we have to address real risks. Obviously, we have to talk about upside too. We have to be realistic with upside, though. We can't just... throw out random numbers, make random claims.
And if and when we do, we have to be FTC compliant. OK, when we talk about upside, we want the upside to be just as I've talked about with all these things. The real upside that the person wants. Okay? If I get you to a million dollars a month and you fucking hate your life. Because I didn't set the proper expectation of the time you'd invest. And I kept telling you, 'I'm like, dude— guys who had a million dollars a month, like this is how they behave. This is what they do.
This is what they think. These are their routines.' You remember how you articulated how you wanted things to be? Thank you. Think about that. It's like that's a part of 'this is an example.' It's an expectation. That's all it is. You have to make sure that the upside that's articulated to the individual, like if you tell me, 'Oh, dude—I can lower your tax burden from $92,000 a month to like $30,000 a month. I wouldn't believe you. I'd be like, 'How the fuck are you going to do that?' You know what would be cool, though? If you said, 'I could take your $92,000 a month down to like $60,000 a month. Even if you could actually take it to 30 grand a month. Does that make sense? I could make a claim. Like guys. In my agency, just so you understand how I actually play the game, I have to get people to a million a month net. Trackable back to me. To hit 100k a month in rev share payouts. You understand what that means, right? Just from that inference alone. That means, yeah, I'm out here always talking about hitting a million dollars a month. Well, what I actually have to do is I have to take people to upwards of like two million a month or sometimes three million a month. But how believable is that? Huh? Not very believable, right? A million a month. Even that. That's hard for some people to wrap their head around.
But to me, that's actually a smaller claim that's more realistic in comparison to what I actually have to do when we work with a business. We don't audit a business based on their probability to hit a million a month gross. We audit a business that we're going to work with based on their probability to far exceed that and hit a million a month net. You get what I'm saying? Okay. Downside. So realistic downside. What's the downside of the offer, Ed? The downside of the offer is that you don't do anything, get nothing, no results. And do I get money back? For what the true and real things are that you must talk about.
And by the way, this is all for marketing automation and like pre-call sequencing, pre-webinar sequencing. This is the stuff that you'd want to talk to people about. Before they show up to the call. These are the things that make them buy. Does that make sense? Okay, I'll move through these last ones quickly. Real scarcity. I'll give you a great one. You guys ready? Brian, what'd you used to pay? $1,500 a month. You paid 15K a year. What did you pay most recently to join after you decided to come back in? $27,000 up front. What is that called right there? That's real scarcity. Guess what would happen if Brian would have joined in a week later? What'd you pay, Ed? $36,000. He paid $36,000. Do you know what people pay right now, Ed, if they want to do that same thing you did and pay annually? Better be more. 44 grand. Is it still worth every penny? Abso-fucking-lutely. If you make back a measly 44 grand out of this group, please ask me for a fucking refund. Shouldn't be so small. Here's my point, though. That's real scarcity, is it not?
Am I full of shit when I tell you I'm going to raise my price? You think it's ever going down again? Call me out on my bluff. Somebody. Anybody? We're going up. Again, next price hike, $7,500 a month. Let another one person join in at $5,000 a month. It's over. They'll never see that price again. $7,500 a month from that point forward. Does that make sense? Good for you that you locked in whatever rate you got. Because you're grandfathered in on that. But you can get Brian'd, and if you leave, and then you inevitably want to come back. Well, you're paying whatever the current rate is. That's real scarcity. You see what I'm saying? You got to actually have legitimate reasons. And I mean legitimate reasons.
Ed, you pulled the trigger at the end of 2024, right? Literally January 1st at midnight, while everybody else is out fucking off doing New Year's Eve stuff. You know what I came home and did, Justin? Jacked up the fucking price. At midnight on the spot, called Leo, hey, happy new year. Bryce in the inner circle's five Gs a month now. You know, we still had like three or four people join in, literally in the first two days of the month. You see what I'm saying? That's real scarcity. What's your offer as real scarcity? Why do I got to do it now? That's the question. Why do I got to do it now? What's the real reason for that? I can quit alcohol tomorrow, James. I mean, I don't need to do it today. I got a party I got to go to tonight. Why do I need to do it now?
I don't believe you. She loves me. See, it's a real reason. Price, in my opinion, is one of your biggest levers. And it doesn't have to be, by the way, like what I just gave you as an example with the inner circle. It doesn't have to be something that just sticks for everybody ongoing in perpetuity. It could be, okay, you come into our funnel today. You got two weeks to pull the trigger on this thing. You don't pull the trigger by then, I don't give a fuck whether you want to buy after two weeks or not at that current rate. We're charging you this instead. After two weeks, we want to encourage you. What did I call you about, Shane? You're a great example of that.
Tell the people, Shane, what did I do to you in our buying process for the inner circle? He was on his way to dinner, and he said that... If you don't decide within an hour, and you think that I'm going to let you be a part of this community of A-players, it says you come into a fly-by. Yeah, I said, go fuck yourself. You're not joining into the group if you don't join by the time I'm done with dinner. Why did I say that, though? No, you had the money. We knew we could help you. And we knew that we needed to deploy some real scarcity to get you to pull the trigger. And we meant every fucking word of it. You didn't pull the trigger, I wouldn't let you in a room of people like this.
Not a chance, but you demonstrated that you had the right traits and characteristics. So we let you in. You did it. To be fair to you, you did it while I was at dinner too. GOOD FOR YOU. You paid off though, right? Yeah. Shane has grown tremendously as both a man and a business owner being a part of this group. He'll attest to that any day of the fucking week. But here's my point. I had to use real scarcity to get him in. Sometimes you have to deploy multiple variables in the trust flywheel. Sometimes it's just one. Does that make sense? Again, what's the real reason that I'm going to do it today? Can't be made up. Can't be some bullshit like, oh, dude, your wife's going to divorce you if you don't stop drinking.
It's like, Elliot, would your wife divorce you if you didn't stop drinking? You can give a fuck about that. It's like the one thing that probably did hit him was seemed like the discipline angle. Where it was, dude, I mean, you're coming off as a highly undisciplined individual. Is that how you want to be? That's what he brought up on the following call with me. He said. Dude, I've gotten control of this thing like I'm not a man out of control—I'm a man in control. That worked fucking wonders on him. Had nothing to do with scarcity. Had to do with calling him out on one of these. That makes sense? All right, next one, safety in numbers. Three trophies in front of you for hitting a million dollars a month.
How do you feel about your probability of hitting a million dollars a month if that's your goal? To make you feel better? Does it make you feel like you're in the right place? Yeah, by the way, these are just from December. We give out these trophies damn near every mastermind because one of you guys hits a million a month for the first time. We've given away a trophy for a guy who hits $6 million in a month. You're sitting amongst people right now that are a part of the literal smallest... probability to get to that point in business on earth. And there's 15 of you that have hit a measly million dollars a month. How safe do you feel in that kind of environment? You're around the right folks.
See what I mean? If you're the lone big dog in the group, how do you think that'd make you feel here in comparison? Hmm? How would you feel if you're the only one here with like a large following? Probably wouldn't feel at home, huh? Safety in numbers: I got to let people know. Here's who you're around when you join into this. It's like, dude— if I want to stop drinking alcohol, and you tell me I'm legitimately. Like, around, like, people that are like me that are doing it, too. It's like, I'll take you up on that any day of the week. I'd be like, 'Sounds fucking sweet.' You're telling me there's other people like me that are in there that relate to my actual problems?
How many of you, when you talk amongst each other, find actual value in what you talk about amongst yourselves? Like you've met somebody here where you're like, 'That guy solved a real problem for me' or gave me some perspective that, you know, really added to me. See what I mean? It's like there's safety in numbers. It can't just be you. You get what I'm saying? Now, this would be a great example of leveraging like testimonials, case studies. But remember. This is your new rule. Any singular time you're going to ever share a case study or a testimonial. You have to have the person you share. Be somebody who's willing to get contacted by other people. Because that's what's actually going to make your testimonials believable. Does that make sense?
Okay, last one, grand narrative. You know, just recently. Let's talk to one of the Inner Circle members named Rohan. Rohan's an impressive young man, 21 years old. He helps people raise capital as well. And Rohan was sharing with me that. He had a real estate syndicate. That was struggling to get people to close. Who here right now? thinks that real estate is the best investment you could make with your money. It gets you the best return out of everywhere else that you could put cash. Raise your hand if you think that. One person. What does that say about the grand narrative going through the minds of people? about real estate. Like you heard when Aleem made that. Radically wrong comment about the political landscape creating fear.
I then said, 'Hey, everybody, raise your hand if you're excited about Big Daddy Trump coming back.' Everybody was like, 'Fucking yeah, dude.' It's the golden era. You could feel the money tingling in the air. You know? But think about this. Is Trump in any way, shape, or form hyping up real estate? There's no grand narrative currently. That says... that just whispers in your ear. You should invest in real estate. There's nothing currently whispering that. There's no whisper in somebody's ear that says. Start an Amazon business. You see? So what does that mean? A part of your responsibility is. You have to tie what you do into the grand narrative. There's always a macro story being told. Like right now is an example.
One of the people we're giving the Million a Month trophy to is James. Stop drinking alcohol. Tell me right now. How often? Have you heard about that in the most recent, like, call it five years? The whisper is in your favor. Stop drinking alcohol. Wellness. Cold blood. Saunas. Eggs and steak and butter. You see? It's like, dude. As a person who's a little fat, do you know what I felt? I saw in real time an unparalleled amount of fat shaming when Big Daddy Trump got re-elected officially. I was like, 'Oh man.' This is the time to be fit. This is it. The grand narrative is whispering in my ear. Shed those pounds, Jeremy. Go into the deficit. You know, the grand narrative controls so much of your decision making.
For what you choose to do. What you choose to not do. Like, dude, the IRS commissioner just recently stepped down, Ed. He resigned. You know, Big Daddy Trump, he repealed the 87,000 new IRS representatives. That makes me feel like I might not get audited. That makes me feel like I might be able to give Ed some money and take a risk. You see what I'm saying? But if that wasn't the... grand narrative. That Ed gets to play into, or the grand narrative. That James gets to play into naturally. What does that mean we have to take responsibility for? As the offer owner. As the business owner. It means we have to tie what we do into the bigger picture. We have to tie what we do and create reasoning into the macro narrative that everybody lives through.
Does that make sense? Like right now. Tell me if I'm tripping out. I don't think. Like, I'm 30 years old. I don't know if this is the case or not. Maybe the older people in here can share some perspective. This feels like the golden age. I feel like these next four years, I'm going to get so unbelievably rich, so much more than I am right now. You know, it just feels like the perfect time. I don't even know what I'm gonna do to get so much richer besides what I already do and amplify it. But it feels like there's these political windows in the past that come along occasionally. That just create monstrous amounts of wealth.
You know, and this feels like I've seen so many comparisons to things like the roaring 20s and like all this crazy shit going on about this is just the golden age. And everybody I talk to, they all collectively agree. And it just, I don't know what it is, just feels like this is the time where there's going to be one of those political windows that just opens up with huge opportunities. Cash is getting thrown around. Right after he got elected, I had so many clients that just had windfalls of cash come in immediately. I had a roofing client, my first client ever. Guy withheld an $8 million check from him if Kamala was going to win. Hands it right over to him the day after Trump gets inaugurated, gets elected in.
It's like my point—I'm trying to make— not to sit here and be political, but my point is: How are you currently tying into the grand narrative of that? It's like I'm over here telling people they can hit million dollar months. People believe it more than ever before. There's a much higher probability of that being real for people. Think about how awful it would be to make a claim like that in a time where people feel financially constrained. They don't see money freely moving around. They think money's being locked up and not spent, and people are being conservative with their cash. You see the differences in how much more probable it is to make a claim? In a certain macro narrative. Think about your claim right now. Does it fit into the big picture?
Or do you have to tie it into the big picture through storytelling? You can't undermine this point. I left it as the last point for a very key reason. I find this to be so consistently in favor of a company's momentum. Or dramatically reducing the probability of them hitting big numbers as a result of going with or against the grand narrative. The companies that always make the most tie into the big picture that everybody currently thinks. Does that make sense? Like, take this as an example. Who in here has been fucked by Stripe? Raise your hand. Okay, well our sponsor here today, Yash, who runs Fanbases. They've become the... favorite of the group recently. And thank you very much, Yash, for being here with us today. He's the owner of Fanbasis.
They do incredible support. They have. Arguably, great rates in comparison to Stripe. They don't fuck people. And unlike WAP, I found out WAP is just like a wrapper of Stripe. Okay, Fanbasis, what do you call it when you're your own uh payment processor? You called it like a like a A what? Yeah, so he's like a legitimate payment processor in comparison to WAP, which is like a Stripe wrapper. So my point being, it's like a good example. Raise your hand again if you've been fucked by Stripe. Keep it up. Grand narrative. How many of you right now are probable to sign up with fan bases if you haven't done so already? Because you've been fucked by Stripe and they can take better care of you. Think about that.
You see what I'm saying as an example? The grand narrative. Is what's going to drive you. To take that action. Do you see what I'm saying? If Stripe cradled you like a baby and took great care of you. Would you be as probable to do it? You see what I'm saying? How do you tie what you do into the grand narrative successfully? Now listen. There's eight of these in total. I think that these should be the first eight emails in the marketing automation sequence. From what I've experienced with the people we've tested this with, again and again and again, when executed in a way that actually represents the truth.
